coredata.dc:description: Recently, optical chemical sensors have been used to simplify traditional methods of chemical analyses. Indeed, sensing strategies based on the photoluminescence properties of nanomaterials need to be well-understood, since there are several nanomaterials with different features regarding their applicability in the optical sensor field. This review summarizes the main 0D, 1D and 2D nanomaterials used in sensing devices, with a special look in their optical properties and different methods of synthesis, as well as a briefing overview related to their colloidal stability, chemical structure and kinetic and thermodynamic parameters. In addition, the analytical performances of different optical sensing strategies are also highlighted, in which pattern recognition algorithms and the development of polymeric-based films are tools used to contribute for the application of new optical chemical sensors with exceptional sensibility and selectivity. Furthermore, this review would give some insights into advances development of optical chemical sensors, using nanomaterials of different dimensionalities.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, optical chemical sensors have been used to simplify traditional methods of chemical analyses. Indeed, sensing strategies based on the photoluminescence properties of nanomaterials need to be well-understood, since there are several nanomaterials with different features regarding their applicability in the optical sensor field. This review summarizes the main 0D, 1D and 2D nanomaterials used in sensing devices, with a special look in their optical properties and different methods of synthesis, as well as a briefing overview related to their colloidal stability, chemical structure and kinetic and thermodynamic parameters. In addition, the analytical performances of different optical sensing strategies are also highlighted, in which pattern recognition algorithms and the development of polymeric-based films are tools used to contribute for the application of new optical chemical sensors with exceptional sensibility and selectivity. Furthermore, this review would give some insights into advances development of optical chemical sensors, using nanomaterials of different dimensionalities.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Sensors have revolutionized many sectors of human life due to their great ability to detect a wide range of vital analytes [1]. Sensors can be defined as a device that responds to a physical stimulus and transmits a resulting impulse as the response. In this sense, optical chemical sensors are devices used to measure an analyte's physical or/and chemical properties into a measurable signal, such as fluorescence emission or absorption of light. These devices are usually sensitive enough to detect a single chemical or biological molecule, and the magnitude of the measurable signal is proportional to the concentration of the analyte [2,3].

Many research groups have been used nanotechnology to design and engineer new structures. In this regard, nanomaterials emerge as excellent candidates for acting in chemical sensing due to quantum dimension and surface effects conferring intrinsic physicochemical properties [4]. Carbon-based nanomaterials are worth-mentioned due to chemical versatility, easy-of-handling, and biocompatibility, allowing the development of robust sensing platforms, and improving their resistance to several mediums, for example, temperature and ionic strength [5,6]. On the other hand, inorganic nanomaterials, such as mesoporous silica nanoparticles, iron oxide, silver and gold nanoparticles, presented some advantageous features including high rigidity, chemical inertness, uniformity, and unlimited possibilities of surface functionalization [7,8]. Indeed, all these outstanding particles have a unique property in common, the nanometric dimension.

Materials with a dimension between 1 and 100 nm can be classified as nanomaterials. There is still another classification considering the predominance of this scale as a function of the spatial dimension of the nanostructure: zero-dimensional (0D), nanomaterials in which the three dimensions are restricted to nanoscale and none of the dimensions are larger than 100 nm; one-dimensional (1D), nanostructures in which one of the dimensions are outside of the nanoscale (one dimension larger than 10 nm); two-dimensional (2D), in which two dimensions are outside of the nanoscale (two dimensions larger than 100 nm); and [9] three dimensional (3D), not confined in the nanoscale in any dimension [10–12]. Fig. 1 summarizes these three classes of nanomaterials and their most representative structures.

Besides dimension, for the development of an optical chemical sensor based on nanomaterials, a transducer is also required for converting the response obtained through the interaction between the target analyte and the sensing element into a measurable signal, corresponding to the concentration of the analyte [13]. This transducer can be understood as a tool, which converges energy into information, resulting in a measurable signal. Some analytical instruments such as spectroscopic methods can be applied for this purpose [14]. For instance, near-infrared (NIR) [15], Raman [16], Ultraviolet–Visible (UV–Vis) [17] and fluorescence spectroscopies [18] are commonly employed techniques for the development of sensing strategies.

There is still the possibility of developing colorimetric sensors, in which it is possible to detect the presence of the analyte only by changing the color of the system [19]. Thereby, some authors propose the development of thin films containing the nanomaterials cited herein [20–22]. These nanocomposites can be applied as transduction signals of the phenomenon of plasmonic resonance surface on carbon nanomaterials [20,23], or proving the change in the color of the system in colorimetric assays [24,25], and as well as variations in the fluorescence intensity or displacement of the band of emission of nanoprobes [26,27]. Fig. 2 presents a graph of scientific works regarding the search for the keywords ‘nanomaterials’ and ‘optical sensing’, demonstrating a growing interest in this field.

As can be observed, it indicates that every day, many researchers are looking for new synthetic routes and applications of nanomaterials as optical chemical sensors, including in the different sensing strategies above mentioned. The main objective of this review is to discuss and summarize the aspects involved in the development of optical chemical sensors based on 0D, 1D and 2D nanomaterials. After the introduction part, in Section 2 we reviewed nanomaterials structures according to their respective dimensions, detailing their properties, especially optical. In Section 3, we presented different sensing platforms, showing their main features, such as chemometric tools and algorithms. Finally, in Section 4 we brief the perspectives of nanomaterials for the sensing field and we the new trends.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, properties and synthesis of nanomaterials for optical sensing
ce:section.ce:para: 0D, 1D and 2D nanomaterials are represented by a wide class of nanoparticles, nanotubes and nanosheets, whose distinct chemical structures may define special properties (quantum effect, plasmonic effect, surface charge and many others), which can be exploited for chemical sensors. The diversity of nanomaterials is mainly attributed to the variety of precursors and synthetic strategies. Different methods have been used to obtain nanomaterials and these choices influence their final properties, providing versatility according to the desire application.

The synthesis of nanomaterials can be classified in two approaches: top-down and bottom-up methods. Fig. 3 summarizes the main methods applied in each approach. In top-down methods, nanomaterials are prepared from larger precursors with bulk structures, meanwhile in bottom-up approaches nanomaterials are synthesized from small molecules or atoms combined [28]. In other words, the bottom-up method uses small organic chemicals, for example, citric acid and urea, or even biological materials such as biological waste or animal and plant sub-products, which are made through chemical conversion to nanoparticles. Alternatively, in a top-down process, a large pure carbon compound, such as carbon nanotubes or graphite, is fragmented into nanoparticles [29,30].

Many techniques have been developed to synthesize nanomaterials with controlled size and shape, as well as dimensional and structure, using physical or chemical principles. For these purposes the main methods used are:(i) electrochemical [31], (ii) hydrothermal [32], (iii) solvothermal [33], (iv) chemical vapor deposition [34] and (v) laser pyrolysis [35]. Nanomaterials with different chemical structures can have their optical and electronic properties adjustable depending on the reaction conditions. General properties such as surface area, which can be increased, and particle size, which can result in the quantum confinement effect [36–38] can also be manipulated. Table 1 summarizes some of the nanomaterials presented in this section and highlights the main information about synthesis methods.

In zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials, all three dimensions are confined to the nanoscale (<100 nm). Common 0D nanomaterials are liposomes, polymeric nanoparticles, metallic nanoparticles, magnetic nanoparticles and quantum dots [39]. The use of these nanostructures has aroused great interest from researchers, in numerous areas of knowledge, given their unique optical, electronic and surface properties [40].

One of the main nanoparticles widely explored for various biomedical and industrial applications are the Quantum Dots (QDs) [41]. In this regard, it is worth mentioning QDs have attracted strong interest among researchers in recent years due to their unique properties such as adjustable optical and electronic properties [42], exceptional photophysical properties [41], high quantum yield [43], photostability [44] and a wide region of the excitation spectrum with narrow emission bands [45].

QDs are known as new-generation nanometer-sized inorganic semiconductor crystals. Generally, they are defined as nanoparticles of semiconductor material that undergo confinement in the three spatial dimensions, and are generally composed by elements from groups II to VI, III to IV, or IV to VI with sizes ranging from 2 to 10 nm [46]. Structurally, QDs consist of a semiconductor core coated with a shell and a lid that allows better solubility in aqueous buffers [47]. The particles of this nanomaterial exhibit different spectroscopic properties depending on the size, structure, and surface property. Thus, a striking feature of these materials is the dependence of their absorption and emission properties with their sizes, i.e., changes in the size of nanoparticles can be responsible for changes in transition energy and, consequently, changes in optical properties [48].

The main property of QDs is the quantum confinement, which arises when the de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron becomes larger than the dimension of the nanostructure and electrons are confined in one, two, or three spatial dimensions [49]. This behavior increases considerably the functional properties, especially the photoluminescence, magnetic and electro-electronic properties [50], which allows these nanostructures to be used in a wide variety of applications [51]. The confinement effect is responsible for the absorption and emission in the visible region and near regions, such as ultraviolet and near-infrared, presented by these materials [48].

Quantum dimensional confinement can improve the optoelectronic properties of QDs, increasing their photoluminescence [52]. QDs are unique in this respect once they are less prone to deeper structural defects and tend to segregate surface defects. It was observed, for example, that confined quantum systems can increase the excitonic emission of QDs, but still, have emissions related to surface defects. As defects are on the surface, they can be easily accessed, and passivation strategies can offer opportunities to control, as well as adjust these properties, particularly emission properties, greater stability and resistivity relative to oxygen and water with the added advantage of bandgap tuning [53]. In addition, QDs have unique optical and electronic properties such as intense and bright fluorescence. Different types of QDs can be excited with the same wavelength of light and their narrow emission bands can be detected simultaneously for multiple assays [45]. Therefore, there is a growing interest in the development of QDs for sensing applications. Sensors based on quantum dots find use in detecting ions, organic compounds (proteins, sugars, volatile substances), as well as bacteria and viruses.

In the work by Huangfu and Feng, CsPbBr3 QDs were synthesized and used to detect various solvents with different polarities [54]. Good linear relationships between CsPbBr3 QDs fluorescence intensity and volume of polar solvents, such as acetone, methanol, and ethanol, were separately obtained, proving the viability of perovskite quantum dots as a sensor for total polar materials (TPM). CsPbBr3 QDs were then used for the detection of TPM in edible oils. With increasing TPM content, the fluorescence intensity of the CsPbBr3 QDs proportionally decreased without any photoluminescence peak shifts. All results indicated that CsPbBr3 QDs can be used as an innovative fluorescent sensor for rapid TPM detection of edible oils with great practical applications.

Another class of QDs reported in the literature are the h-BN quantum dots (h-BNQDs) [55]. These nanoparticles can be functionalized by few methods such as hydrothermal or microwave irradiation treatment of h-BN nanosheets in organic solution [56,57]. On the other hand, laser ablation is a simple, fast, and ‘green’ approach for the synthesis of various nanomaterials, including h-BNQDs. Nguyen et al. synthesized h-BNQD with tunable PL properties, using laser ablation [58]. These authors reported the preparation of three BNQDs with tunable photoluminescence from ultraviolet to the green region by femtosecond laser ablation of h-BN powder in different solutions. Carbene zigzag edges, as well as abundant functional groups, were generated on the surface of BNQDs by one step.

In the work by Liang et al., a dual-signal fluorescent sensor was developed with molybdenum disulfide quantum dots (MoS2 QDs) with blue fluorescence (433 nm) and cadmium telluride quantum dots (CdTe QDs) with fluorescence yellow (573 nm), for the detection of tetracycline (TET) in milk [59]. With the addition of TET, the fluorescence of MoS2/CdTe was quenched by photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and the fluorescence of CdTe QDs was quenched more obviously than MoS2 QDs. In addition, the dual signal sensor was applied for detection of TET in milk samples with recovery of 95.53–104.22% and relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 5%, indicating strong application potential with excellent reproducibility and stability.

Another example of the application of QDs in optical sensing is the use of CdS quantum dots, coated with aqueous polyethyleneimine, in the detection of toxic metal ions suggested by Das, Rakshit e Datta [60]. In this work, Pb2+ binds strongly to the surface of the QDs, facilitating the ultra-fast transfer of electrons. As a result, a severe (∼90%) photoluminescence extinction is observed. Selectivity in this detection process is controlled by the quality of the QD surface, the nature of the coating binder, and its metal ion binding properties. The engineering of these factors is crucial for the development of selective and efficient QD-based metal ion sensors.

In the last two decades, QD synthesis methodologies have made substantial progress. As earlier mentioned, the bottom-up method has significant advantages which includes less time-consuming and allows easy modification of the surface state and composition of the QDs [61]. In this sense, bottom-up methods are the most common choice in the literature. This synthetic process can be performed using several techniques (electrochemical, solvothermal and others). However, the most commonly used technique for preparing QDs is the hydrothermal method due to its simplicity, low economic cost, and relatively safe processing [49]. Furthermore, the size and fluorescent properties of QDs can be easily controlled by changing precursors or experimental conditions. This characteristic is one of the main effects of the hydrothermal technique, as can synthesize crystals with better quality and growth, maintaining reasonable control over their composition [62]. The hydrothermal method is based on the reaction of aqueous solution vapors with solid material at high pressure and temperature, leading to the deposition of small particles [63,64].

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are a class of organic nanoparticles with features of QDs. They were discovered accidentally in 2004 during the purification process of carbon nanotubes by electrophoresis [65]. Compared with QDs, CQDs emerge as a new type of photoluminescent nanomaterial that offers similar attributes, such as size less than 10 nm, wavelength-dependent luminescence emission, resistance to photodegradation, ease of bioconjugation, low-cost production, colloidal stability, elemental abundance, and low environmental and biological toxicity [66].

The photoluminescent properties of CQDs are related to their dimension (1 and 10 nm in diameter). Their chemical structure generally consists of an almost spherical structure formed by carbon sheets with sp2 or sp3 hybridization [67]. According to John and colleagues, CQDs may have chromophore groups or surface defects, such as nitrogen atoms present in the edge of the nanoparticles [68]. Given these characteristics, CQDs have great potential for applications, acting in photocatalysis [69], electrocatalysis [70], optoelectronic devices [71], and optical chemical sensors [72].

In general, a carbon source is requested to obtain CQDs and sources of heteroatoms, such as oxygen, nitrogen, or other groups to enable the surface functionalization and to obtain different chemical structures and photoluminescent properties. In the case of precursors or auxiliary agents with heteroatoms (N, S, B, P, etc.), the atomic doping of the CQDs can be performed, which can efficiently regulates the state of the electronic and chemical surface characteristics of carbon nanomaterials [73].

Microwave synthesis provides advantages in the nanoparticle synthesis such as simplicity, low cost, short reaction time, and the ability to generate a variety of CQDs in a single step [74]. In this method, microwaves are used to improve the reaction to obtain uniform heating and, therefore, enhance chemical reactions [75]. On the other hand, thermal decomposition, also known as thermolysis, is one of the most common synthesis methods to obtain highly fluorescent CQDs [76]. This method involves the process of dehydration of the precursor molecule and includes the advantages of low economic cost, speed, and simplicity in synthesis.

Among several bottom-up methods, hydrothermal synthesis is the most widely adopted method to synthesize zero-dimensional (0D) nanomaterials, such as CQDs [34]. The hydrothermal method is a simple and versatile method for the synthesis of organic nanomaterials, in which water is used as solvent, under conditions of high temperature and pressure. Furthermore, it is possible to modulate the characteristics of the carbon nanoparticles by adjusting the parameters temperature, pressure, or concentration of the precursor [77]. In addition, this technique draws the attention of many scientists, considering tremendous advantages, such as low economic cost and relatively safe processing [49].

Solvothermal synthesis is another more general synthetic method that can also be effective in the synthesis of CQDs. As in the hydrothermal method, it carried out in a stainless-steel autoclave, however, instead of using water to solubilize the precursor, in the solvothermal method, other solvents can be applied [78]. In the solvothermal method, by fitting certain experimental parameters, including temperature, time, and solvent, it is possible to precisely control the size, shape distribution, and crystallinity of the obtained nanomaterials [79,80]. Depending on the solvent used, different emission colors may exist. This is because the solvent controls the dehydration and carbonization process of the precursor and determines the particle size and the domain size of the sp2 conjugate, thereby, obtaining a tunable fluorescent multicolor emission CQD [81].

In the work of Ding et al. for example, a series of CQDs with tunable emission from 443 to 745 nm were obtained just by setting the reaction solvents in a one-pot solvothermal path. The distinct optical characteristics of these CQDs are based on their differences in particle size and content of graphitic nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups, which can be modulated by controlling the dehydration and carbonization processes during solvothermal reactions [82].

CQDs can have a strong wavelength-dependent luminescence emission. These dependencies can be adjusted by employing different synthetic conditions [29]. Thus, the luminescence mechanism is important to guide the synthesis of CQDs with emission fluorescence. CQDs are gaining increasing importance as photoluminescent markers [83]. Currently, three theories seek to explain the luminescence of CQDs: quantum confinement effect, the surface state, and molecular fluorescence [84]. Therefore, CQDs can emit light in different regions of the visible spectrum, depending on their size [85], effects on their surface caused by functional groups [86], and fluorescent molecules connected on the surface or interior of CQDs [87].

The Quantum Yield (QY), a comparison parameter for the photoluminescence of CQDs, is calculated by the ratio between the number of photons absorbed and the number of photons emitted by a material [88]. To calculate QY, the following Eq. (1) is used:
(1)

where QY refers to the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorption intensity at the excitation wavelength, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and the subscript s is related to the values of the standard fluorescent molecule 77.

The QY of the CQDs is lower when compared to QDs. However, the functionalization on the surface of the CQDs or a careful choice of precursor with specific functional groups may reflect in a greater photoluminescence efficiency. Functionalization consists of organic reactions of substitution, elimination or addition, providing the formation of functional groups in a controllable way [89]. With functionalization, CQDs can coordinate with some metal ions through their abundant surface functional groups, thus exerting a vital influence on the morphology and particle size of the final products. Furthermore, functionalized CQDs have good water or oil solubility when different functional groups are introduced [73].

Functionalized CQDs with high fluorescence signals can act as optical chemical sensors. These fluorescence-based sensors have added a lot of attention to routine analysis due to their simplicity, chemical stability, and usability [67]. The versatile surface modification from the presence of surface groups, good solubility in polar solvents, and extensive optical absorption across the entire visible wavelength and near-infrared regions make CQDs highly potent in sensing applications [90]. Several works in the literature demonstrate the effectiveness of these nanomaterials for applications in sensing. For instance, Gao et al., synthesized CQDs for detection of Au3+ in aqueous solution for bioimaging in cells [91].

Surface plasmons (SPs), a name attributed by Stern and Ferrell in 1960s, refer to the coherent and collective oscillations of conduction electrons on the material surface under the stimulation of quantized energies, including photons, electrons and phonons [92]. The nanoparticles which presented SPs are called plasmonic nanoparticles (PNPs) and exhibit the phenomenon of Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR), defined by the interaction of light with these nanoparticles. The theory and experimental approach adopted to explain LSPR were firstly developed for metal nanoparticles since the physical size scale is much smaller than the light wavelength used [93]. PNPs have increasingly been used to fabricate colorimetric sensors because of their strong LSPR extinction in the visible light region [94]. In this class, the prominent representatives are the gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), gold nanocluster (AuNCs), silver nanocluster (AgNCs) and the silver nanoparticles (AgNPs).

AuNPs, also known as gold colloids, are the most stable metal nanoparticles and display specific optical properties not observed in bulk-size materials. Given this scenario, the intrinsic properties of AuNPs offer benefits to the fabrication of smart sensors [95]. AuNPs are the most preferred nanoparticles for sensing since they possess many desirable features which make them a potential candidate for this purpose. The primary requirement for the colorimetric detection is the high SPR activity which determines the sensitivity of the colorimetric sensor, as it will be shown in section 3 of this review [96].

Regarding synthesis method, AuNPs are obtained by numerous physico-chemical and biological routes [97]. The classical Turkevich method allows the synthesis of highly stable AuNPs around 20 nm size, wherein the nanoparticle surface is coated with citrate molecules, presenting a negative surface charge avoiding particle aggregation. The biological synthesis of nanoparticles is a safe, dynamic, and energy-efficient method of producing nanoparticles. Metabolites (proteins, fatty acids, sugars, enzymes, and phenolic compounds) found in these sources play a significant role in the bioreduction of metallic ions to NPs and their stability. Furthermore, AuNPs biologically generated are more stable than those generated using other methods [98].

On the other hand, the most popular strategy to produce AuNPs in solution is based on the gold salts chemical reduction in the presence of stabilizing molecules. This method required purification steps to separate chemical products and unreacted factors from the AuNPs, meanwhile, in pulsed laser ablation in liquid there is no need for any chemical assistance to form noble metal NPs. Instead, it provides high purity for the prepared NPs [99]. In another work, Toro-González et al. evaluated the formation of gold nanoparticles by the radiolytic synthesis in a continuous flow using a millifluidic reactor [100]. A mixture of gold ions solution, branched polyethyleneimine, and ethylene glycol yielded gold nanoparticles with a mean size of 3.4 ± 1.0 nm after irradiation with an X-ray irradiator.

A variation of gold nanoparticles is the gold nanocluster. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) with a core size below 2 nm are also plasmonic nanoparticles, with characteristic physical and chemical properties. The properties of AuNCs are more prominent and extremely different from their bulk counterparts [101]. One of these properties is the straightforward application of biomolecules for the synthesis of AuNCs. It has been reported that the cage effect of biomolecules can effectively ensure the stability of the NCs, enhances their optical properties and reduces their possible toxicity [102]. In this regard, BSA is a well-studied biomolecule and it has been applied for the synthesis of BSA templated AuNCs, which demonstrated as a selective and sensitive sensors [103,104].

AgNPs are a class of nanoparticles which are mainly used for antimicrobial and anticancer therapy, and biosensors [105]. Although, AuNPs remain the most studied NPs due their good chemical stability and biocompatibility AgNPs offer better results in terms of sensitivity [106]. Recently, Wang et al. have developed silver Ångstrom (Å, 1 Å = 0.1 nm) particles (AgÅPs), which exhibit better biological activity and lower toxicity compared with AgNPs [107]. Jalab et al. synthesized AgNPs by an eco-friendly, economical and more effective approach using Acacia cyanophylla plant extract, as well as to study the effects of the preparation conditions on the size of synthesized nanoparticles and their antibacterial activity [108]. Rajar et al. PVP obtained functionalized Ag nanoparticles (PVP@AgNPs) by chemo-reductive methodology at ambient conditions [109]. In this work, the authors developed and examined PVP capped AgNPs for the sensing of Co2+ ions in aqueous solutions. AgNPs were produced by reducing the silver nitrate in sodium borohydride (NaBH4) aqueous solution. The 0.1% of PVP was used as a capping agent and NaBH4 was used to accelerate the reaction.

The silver-gold bimetallic nanoparticles were further oxidized to form stable silver-gold bimetallic oxide nanoparticles to detect Cr(III) [110]. In this work, AgNPs were prepared using the microwave-assisted method. The saponin powder was employed to act as the capping agent and a dispersing agent during the nanoparticle preparation. Then, silver nitrate solution of 0.01 mol/L was treated with saponin powder dissolved in double-distilled water. In other work, Shehala and co-authors detected the presence of saccharin in the concentration range of 5 × 10−5 mol/L to 5 × 10−1 mol/L through the interference synthesis of gum ghatti (GG) capped silver nanoparticles (GGAgNPs) [111]. The synthesis used sodium borohydride and gum ghatti (GG) as the reducing and capping agents, respectively.

1D nanomaterials are nanomaterials with orientation in a uniform direction [112,113]. This characteristic dimension, together with the possibility of increasing the surface area, allows 1D nanomaterials to be applied in gas and optical sensing [113]. This class includes nanorods, nanowires and nanotubes as typical representatives [114,115]. Herein, these nanostructures will be described and some applications in sensing will be presented.

Nanorods are columns, rods or pillars, with only lateral dimension on the order of tens nanometers. The nanorods growth is feasible through various approaches, such as physical vapor deposition (PVD) [116], chemical vapor deposition (CVD) [117], and solution processing [118]. PVD is the cleanest and most controllable process and can include additional chemical reactions and solid-fluid mechanics to understand other more complex growth processes. Therefore, some authors take the PVD process as the prototype for scientific understanding of nanorod growth [119]. Moreover, if nucleation and growth of nanorods occur in the same solution, the method is coined seedless growth [120].

Furthermore, metal oxide nanorods (ZnO, Fe3O4, NiO, MgO) may be prepared by hydrothermal growth. In this approach, the precipitation of metal oxide nanorods occurs in a supersaturation solution of hydroxyl anions. Homogeneous nucleation may also be performed in an autoclave to favor the nucleation and crystal growth [120]. Recently, an analysis of ZnO-based devices with different nanostructure geometries, anisotropic nanorods and isotropic nanoparticles demonstrated the relevance of the nanostructure geometry and device architecture on the conduction and resistive switching properties [121].

Yin et al. prepared Zn2GeO4: Mn2+, Pr3+ (ZGOMP) nanorods through a one-pot hydrothermal method and applied as an autofluorescence-free biosensor using ZGOMP as a probe and gluconic acid as a quencher to detect prostate-specific antigen (PSA) [122]. ZGOMP nanorods with great afterglow properties were synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The authors confirmed that ZGOMP is easily decomposed under acidic conditions, which results in a significant decrease in the persistent luminescence (PL) signal of ZGOMP as the pH decreases.

Tao et al. developed a quantitative biosensor based on gold nanorods (AuNRs) for sensing glucose [123]. AuNRs with tailorable longitudinal localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) band and large extinction coefficients have high photothermal effects, especially in NIR region. The changing of an aspect ratio of AuNRs resulted in the shift in the LSPR band and the change in the photothermal conversion efficiency. Although AuNRs-based colorimetric biosensors have been successfully developed for qualitative testing by naked eyes, quantitative detection still relies on expensive equipment and skilled operators.

Nanowires (NW) are 1D nanoparticles, they can be formed through many materials, the most relevant are the metallic and polymeric NWs. The first class contains elements or oxides. They are metallic or semiconducting nanostructures with cylindrical morphology, completely filled without any hollows in the structure. If the structure were hollow, it would be a nanotube [124]. Many metal oxides, such as ZnO, SnO2, WO3, NiO, CuO, have been applied in order to show great gas sensing behavior [125].

Of all the NWs preparation methods, those of vapor phase with their general applicability, stand out as the most common route to the synthesis of high-quality nanowires with controllable dimension, composition and shape. Nevertheless, current interests in other growth techniques, especially those based on the solution phase model, have also increased as they offer some advantages [126]. In the specific case of NWs, several peculiarities and interesting possibilities arise, as three-dimensional heterostructures can be prepared. Starting from an NW, a second nucleation can be achieved on the NW external surface resulting in hierarchical or branched heterostructures, increasing the surface-to-volume ratio that is critical for some applications in biosensors. It may be also exploited for electronic devices [127].

Li et al. developed a biosensor based on uniform dimensions high-response silicon nanowire (SiNW) for DNA detection [128]. High-response 120-SiNWs array was fabricated on a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) by the complementary metal-oxide semiconductor (CMOS)-compatible with microfabrication technology. In order to quickly and accurately identify target ctDNA, polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microcavity structure was designed on the top of the sensing area, which directs the solution to the SiNWs. The microfluidic structure effectively reduced the interference caused by the external environment and achieved the rapid transportation of ctDNA molecules.

Zhang et al. prepared electrodes based on Ag nanowires and molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) [129]. The biosensor showed high sensitivity and specificity for the detection of lactate. The MIPs were firstly prepared by electropolymerization of 3-aminophenyl boronic acid with imprints of lactate on the AgNWs coated carbon working electrode. Further, the lactate molecules were rinsed from MIPs to leave the specific binding sites to detect lactate in sweat. Shariati et al. reported an ultrasensitive field-effect transistor (FET) for hepatitis B virus deoxyribonucleic acid (HBV DNA) detection in label free approach and easily reproducible setup [130]. The MoS2 NWs fabrication was materialized through ZnO based on vapor-liquid-solid (VLS) technique.

Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are a representative class of one-dimensional nanomaterial. They have a tubular shape and honeycomb structure, with carbon atoms hybridized in sp2 [131]. The CNTs are divided into single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT), single sheet of graphite and multi-walled nanotubes (MWCNT), and concentrically interwoven nanotubes [132]. Among the main synthesis methods of CNTs, the arc discharge, laser ablation, chemical vapor deposition and flame methods stand out [133].

The unique properties of CNTs, such as high mechanical strength, ultra-light weight, high charge conductivity and a large surface area make them the object of study by several research groups around the world [134,135]. The properties of CNTs are explored by several technological areas, and these nanomaterials are used in electronic devices, catalysis and sensing platforms [131]. This last application is part of the scope of this work and, therefore, will be highlighted throughout this section.

Commonly, CNTs are linked to other materials to obtain superior properties in sensing strategies. For example, Kumar Mittal and Jaiswal [136] filled and coated MWCNTs with silver. The authors observed significant improvements in gross diffusivity, an increase of 242%; in thermal conductivity, an increase of 255%, and in electrical conductivity, an increase of 173%, compared to MWCNTs. The authors tried the hybrid material in I2 detection. The film formed by Ag and MWCNTs proved to be stable and selective in this application, with a detection response of 45% in just 60s at room temperature [136].

Wu and collaborators [137] obtained a composite film formed by MWCNTs and a water-based polyurethane (WPU) matrix. The authors observed that the breaking strength, breaking elongation and initial decomposition temperature of the MWCNT-WPU composite improved by 69.6%, 16.4% and 130 °C, respectively, compared to the pure WPU matrix. Furthermore, it was observed that the WPU matrix improved the exposure rate of MWCNTs due to its uniform dispersion in the composite, which contributed to a better performance in the detection of O3 gas, about 4.1 times compared to pure MWCNTs [137].

Often, CNTs can also be modified or doped by heteroatoms, as in the work of Sedelnikova et al. [138]. The authors modified SWCNTs with sulfur in order to detect nitrogen dioxide gas. Among the main results, the excellent sensitivity and reversibility of the NO2 sensing platform in the concentration range from 1 to 10 ppm. The authors suggested that sulfur acts in the charge transfer process between the SWCNTs and the adsorbed gas molecules, guaranteeing superior results compared to the ones obtained using pure SWCNTs [138].

It is interesting to highlight that CNTs are the most representative materials of the class of nanotubes involved in sensing, but they are not the only ones. Chen and co-authors [139] obtained Fe2O3@Co3O4 composite double-layer nanotubes from easy coaxial electrospinning. The authors produced an ethanol sensing platform, which showed a detection limit of less than 1 ppm at 160 °C. The high sensitivity of the developed sensor was attributed to hollow structures and heterojunctions, which provided the active sites for gas adsorption [139].

Vivekanandan and collaborators [140] obtained ZnO nanotubes (ZNTs) and hybrid ZnO nanotubes interconnected with MoS2 (MoS2-HIZNT). The authors noticed that the hybrid nanomaterial was significantly superior at detecting H2 at concentrations from 10 to 500 ppm at room temperature. Moreover, the higher detection efficiency, around 51.1%, is attributed to the greater active surface area of MoS2-HIZNT compared to ZNTs. Furthermore, it was observed that the MoS2 solution reduced the thickness of the walls of the ZnO nanotubes, which improved the electron mobility of the material and enhanced its application in a sensing platform [140].

2D nanomaterials comprise a wide class of materials, which are well-remembered as graphene and its derivatives. However, different elements other than carbon can form inorganic nanostructures with this dimensionality. Then, the rich chemical nature of 2D nanomaterials will be explored in this section for further applications of these nanomaterials as optical and electrochemical sensors.

Graphene is one of the allotropes of carbon, with a spatial arrangement of a single or multi-layer of sp2 carbon atoms present in a hexagonal lattice [141]. This nanomaterial has become popular because of its excellent physicochemical properties, which include a high surface area [142], mechanical strength [143] and ultra-high electrical conductivity [144]. These properties can be further improved by modifying the surface of graphene by adding functional groups such as hydroxyls, carbonyls and epoxides, obtaining a derivative, known as graphene oxide (GO) [92]. The most commonly known approach today for synthesizing GO is the modified Hummers’ method [145]. The original procedure includes the oxidation of the graphite powder in the presence of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4). However, some works modify this method to obtain different levels of oxidation, from the variation of the reaction time, for example [146,147]. Chen et al. modified GO with chitosan and were able to successfully detect Cd2+ ions, developing a sensor with low manufacturing cost and high sensitivity [148].

The surface of GO also can be reduced, obtaining another nanostructure, the reduced graphene oxide (rGO). The reduction consists of the chemical reaction for decreasing the content of oxygen atoms present in the GO [149]. Regarding the methods of obtaining rGO, reducing agents such as hydrazine [150], sodium borohydride [151] or hydroquinone [152] are commonly employed. However, the chemical method involves substances that are toxic to healthy humans and harmful to the environment [153]. Thus, thermal reduction is an alternative method commonly used to obtain rGO, ensuring the attainment of nanomaterial with excellent properties and friendly to the environment [154]. Furthermore, the hydrothermal method has been pointed out as a viable way to apply rGO as sensors. Chinnathambi et al. first synthesized GO by the Hummers’ method and later rGO was obtained by hydrothermal treatment of the GO dispersion at 160 °C, for 6 h. The authors then applied the rGO obtained as a potentiometric and chemoresistive pH sensor [155].

A perfect graphene structure is formed by a single layer of carbon atoms arranged in two dimensions [156]. However, obtaining this type of structure is an arduous task. Some recurrent methods used in the literature are mechanical exfoliation [157], chemical exfoliation [158], and chemical vapor deposition [159]. In a typical mechanical exfoliation process, graphene sheets are obtained by layer-by-layer removal of graphite, breaking the Van der Waals attraction forces between adjacent layers [160]. Chemical exfoliation of graphite in liquid phase using a homogenizer or ultrasonicator can effectively prevent the structural destruction of graphene caused by the oxidation process, making this method very advantageous [158]. On the other hand, chemical vapor deposition offers graphene with a high surface area and few defects, enabling application in electronic devices that require high transparency and high electrical conductivity, such as lab on a chip (LOC) device for sensors [161,162].

Transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) are 2D nanomaterials with general formula MX2 (M = transition metal in Group 4 to Group 10 and X = chalcogen elements S, Se or Te) and a hexagonally packed plane structure, where the metal is sandwiched between two layers of chalcogen atoms [163,164]. Furthermore, interlayers with relatively weak Van der Waals forces connect the single layers of TMDs to form multilayer materials [164].

In addition to the variety of elements, TMDs can have three different phases, shown in Fig. 4 : 3R, 2H and 1T, in which R, H and T represent rhombohedral, hexagonal and trigonal symmetry, respectively [165]. In the 2H phase, nanosheets have strong optical absorption and emission bands in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum. Furthermore, the absorption band can be shifted depending on the size and thickness of the planes [166].

TMDs nanosheets can be prepared by similar methods to those of other 2D nanomaterials: mechanical exfoliation [167], liquid-phase exfoliation [168] and chemical vapor deposition [169], for example. These nanosheets feature adjustable bandgaps and high-quality exfoliation, enabling applications in electronics, optoelectronics and as sensors [170,171]. Jiang et al. obtained few layers of MoS2 by mechanical exfoliation and applied them to detect Hg2+ ions [167]. The authors effectively modulate the electron transport and photoluminescence properties in few-layer MoS2 and obtained rapid electrical detection of Hg2+ ions with a limit of detection (LOD) of 30 pmol/L.

In another study, Zuo and collaborators used WS2 for the detection of Hg2+ and Ag+ with efficient dual-color fluorescent emission [172]. In this work, a simultaneous detection of the analytes was performed, using the excitation wavelengths of 480 and 530 nm for Hg2+ and Ag+, respectively. This sensing showed a high sensitivity with LOD values of 3.3 nmol/L for Hg2+ and 1.2 nmol/L for Ag+ ions.

As carbon's neighbor in the periodic table, boron has a binding capacity as strong as this element. In recent years, studies on small planar boron clusters have increased; especially B36, which is proven to be the first experimental evidence of the viability of 2D boron, called borophene [173]. Furthermore, since the two-dimensional boron (borophene) sheet synthesized in 2015 on Ag substrates, researches with application of this 2D nanostructure have been explored in different fields of condensed matter physics, chemistry, materials science and nanotechnology [174]. Due to its unique physical and chemical properties, borophene has several potential applications, primarily in chemical sensors. Moreover, borophene has high electroactive surface, anisotropic behavior, high electron transport, controlled optical properties, feasibility of deposition on thin films. Therefore, the possibility of generating surface functionalities enables the development of a sensitive smartphone-operated biosensor systems to achieve early-stage Point-of-Care (POC) diagnosis [175–177].

In their work, Hou, Tai and Liu evaluated the application of borophene in a chemoresistive sensor device for gas detection, especially for nitrogen dioxide (NO2) [178]. The sensor featured high sensitivity, fast response, high selectivity, good flexibility, long-term stability and a wide detection range from 200 ppb to 100 ppm, and fast response time of 30 s and 200 s recovery time at room temperature, which are notably superior to those of 2D materials. And the borophene-based sensor has also been proven to have a unique selectivity for NO2 compared to other common gases. In addition, the sensor also features superior flexibility and stability under different bending angles. Thus, demonstrating excellent electronic and sensing characteristics of borophene.

After emerging research with graphene, other inorganic nanomaterials attracted the attention of researchers [179]. Advances in synthetic methods have led to other inorganic 2D nanomaterials, including silicene [180], arsenene [181], germanene [182], antimonene [183] and phosphorene [184,185]. Some synthetic methods have been used to obtain phosphorene. The common top-down methods are mechanical exfoliation using scotch tape or ultrasound-based exfoliation in solution, and among the bottom-up methods, the physical epitaxial growth, wet-chemistry techniques or chemical vapor deposition are used to obtain these nanostructures [186].

Phosphorene possesses an energy gap in the range of 0.6–1.5 eV, enabling application in sensors [179]. In addition, the theoretical simulation shows that phosphorene exhibits higher molecular adsorption energy, which rivals other 2D materials, such as MoS2 and graphene [187]. Saravanan et al. applied a zipper phosphorene nanosheet as a semiconductor with an energy gap of 1.354 eV and it could be deployed as formaldehyde and acetaldehyde sensor [188]. Wang et al. studied the influence of the surface decoration of phosphorene with Ag nanoclusters. These authors showed that the sensitivity of phosphorene can be improved by the decoration with Ag due to the participation of valence electrons of the metal (coming from the s and d electronic orbitals), modulating the charge distribution between the phosphorene and the adsorbed molecules [189].

Nanomaterials containing carbon atoms alternating with nitrogen and with delocalized double bonds are chemically defined as carbon nitrides. This class is represented by formula CxNy, where x and y are any positive real number [190]. The first predicted structure was the β-C3N4 crystalline phase, but other such as α-, g- and cubic-C3N4 were predicted by X-ray diffraction technique [191]. Thus, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a nanomaterial that has been extensively studied due to its excellent characteristics including easy availability, low cost, environmental compatibility and photochemical stability [191,192]. In terms of bond lengths, CN is smaller than C–C resulting in interesting physicochemical, optical and electronic properties [190].

Among the synthesis methods, the chemical approach is usually applied, using precursors that contain already formed C–N bonds, such as dicyandiamide, melamine and related heterocyclic arenes [193]. Freire et al. synthesized the g-C3N4 sheet by a simple thermal exfoliation process [194]. The authors proposed that melamine is formed in condensation processes, in which the structure known as melen is formed and NH3 is released. Only after polymerization of the melen, the chain of g-C3N4 is formed.

To investigate the reaction mechanism of the formation of g-C3N4, Zhang and collaborators used urea and thiourea as precursors and observed that the steps of the synthesis consisted of nucleophilic addition, polycondensation and polymerization [195]. Firstly, urea or thiourea molecules were converted by condensation reactions, and posteriorly, dicyandiamide molecules were directly condensed to melamine. Cai et al. prepared g-C3N4 nanobelts to develop a platform for sensing NO2 gas at room temperature [196]. The authors prepared the nanobelts from the thermal treatment of g-C3N4 bulk and this was obtained by thermal polymerization of melamine. Highly fluorescent g-C3N4 nanobelts with an emission band at 494 nm and 23.6% quantum yield were successfully obtained.

Boron nitride is a nanomaterial that is composed of an equal proportion of boron and nitrogen atoms that can exist in different crystalline phases, such as cubic (c-BN), hexagonal (h-BN), wurtzite (w-BN), and rhombohedral (r-BN) [197]. Hexagonal boron nitride (h-BN), also known as ‘white graphene’, is a 2D nanomaterial with strong covalent sp2 bonds in the plane, which enables excellent chemical stability [198]. Furthermore, h-BN has become a hot spot in nanomaterials science due to its large specific surface area [199], good thermal stability [200], unique electrical [201] and optical properties [202].

Remaining with these excellent properties, Cao et al. used platinum-decorated h-BN nanosheets for dopamine detection [203]. The interaction with the dopamine was found to be weak, and the sensing response is about 0.8. Further, in order to overcome this limitation, the authors decorated the nanosheets with platinum and observed that the detection response increased substantially to 61.2. The results based on computational calculations agreed with the experimental values. In other work, Wang et al. developed a biosensor based on CdS quantum dots anchored h-BN nanosheets and tripodal DNA walker for sensitive detection of miRNA-141 [204]. Interestingly, the porous structure of the h-BN nanosheets favored the formation of specific surface areas, which facilitated its use as a photoelectric substrate for sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermodynamic properties and kinetic parameters in chemical sensing
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of an optical chemical sensor, we must know the chemical nature of the nanoprobe and the analyte. From this interaction, we can investigate the thermodynamic properties and kinetic parameters, obtaining valuable information for the development of the sensing platform. Herein, we list in Fig. 5 such properties and the types of quenching mechanisms that can be inferred from them. With this knowledge in hand, we can answer the following questions: why and how does occur a chemical interaction or reaction between the nanomaterial and the molecule to be detected?

The first question can be answered from two points of view. Initially, one might suggest that product formation leads to a lower energy state, regardless of the steps involved. On the other hand, a chemical reaction can occur, forming the product with the lowest energy barrier; therefore, it depends on the intermediate steps. In the first case, the product formed is thermodynamically more stable and the properties involved are a function of state. In the second case, the kinetically more favored product is formed and the parameters involved are a path function [205].

In chemical sensors, the interaction of the analyte with the sensing platform causes changes in the system, which can be measured by aid of a transducer [13]. These changes can be thermodynamically or kinetically controlled and their understanding can clarify the mechanism by which the analyte interacts with the sensing platform. Herein, the main tools used in the determination of thermodynamic properties and kinetic parameters involved in chemical sensing platforms using nanomaterials will be described.

Yang and collaborators synthesized hydrothermally ErFeO3 nanoparticles [206]. The authors obtained rough nanoparticles with diameters varying from 70 to 110 nm, facilitating the adsorption and desorption of gas molecules. The results showed that ErFeO3 nanoparticles can be used to selectively detect isopropanol in temperatures up to 330 °C. The main mechanism is based on the concentration of gas in the holes formed by the ErFeO3 nanoparticles. In addition, quantum dots are also frequently used in sensing strategies and, as in the work mentioned above, kinetic factors and thermodynamic properties of interaction are fundamental to the description of the sensing process. The main tool for determining thermodynamic properties in these cases is the Stern-Volmer equation, as shown in Eq. (2):

Commonly, the values of I
0 and I are measured at a known concentration Q and temperature T of the quencher. Then the K
SV value is determined, as in the works already reported in the literature [208–210]. Then, under equilibrium conditions, the enthalpy and entropy change for the fluorophore-quencher interaction can be estimated using the Van't Hoff Eq. (3):

(4)
