Additionally, thermodynamic parameters may indicate which quenching mechanism predominates in the interaction between the fluorescent nanoprobe and the analyte. There are two general mechanisms that govern this process: static and dynamic quenching. In the first case, a non-fluorescent complex is formed between the fluorophore and the quencher, usually via electrostatic attraction [211]. In this case, the value of K
SV decreases with increasing temperature [212]. An increase in the ΔS value of the system is also observed. This greater disorder is accompanied by a negative value of ΔH, since the process is exothermic. Consequently ΔG will be negative, indicating the spontaneity of the mixture. In the dynamic quenching process, a collision occurs between the fluorescent nanoparticles and the quencher, resulting in an increase in K
SV with temperature.

Vashisht and collaborators [213] used the method described above to calculate K
SV and the thermodynamic properties involved in the interaction of Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions and phosphate-functionalized graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots Ph-g-CNQDs. The authors performed titrations at four different temperatures and determined ΔH, ΔS and ΔG in each of them, as well as the value of K
SV . It was observed that K
SV increased with temperature, which, according to the authors, it is attributed to the increase in the rate of the bimolecular reaction k
q , caused by the decrease in the activation energy of the process. Furthermore, the authors reported negative ΔG values at all temperatures studied, suggesting that the interaction of the fluorophore with the quencher is a spontaneous process. Thus, the authors proposed that both Fe(II) and Fe(III) ions form a non-fluorescent complex with Ph-g-CNQDs, predominantly obtaining a static mechanism of fluorescence quenching [213].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing strategies based on optical properties of nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: After knowing the synthesis, optical properties and the kinetic and thermodynamic factors involved in the sensing with nanomaterials presented so far, the reader can perceive the richness of applications in the field of chemical analysis. Compared to other traditional methods, the use of spectroscopy techniques combined with suitable nanomaterials can meet the requirements of high precision, short detection time and analysis simplicity [214].

Spectroscopic techniques involve the analysis of ultraviolet, visible and infrared spectra. In many cases, it is not mandatory to apply the totality of the analyzed range and then only select a specific wavelength, usually where there is a greater intensity of the measured signal, considering sensitivity reasons. In this case, for example, turn-off and turn-on sensors only evaluate the quenching or enhancement of fluorescence intensity, respectively [215,216]. On the other hand, it is common to find works that use all the information obtained from spectroscopic measurements to identify and quantify the analytes. In these works, multivariate analysis chemometric tools are used to develop sensor arrays [217,218]. In this section, our study will focus on the application of nanomaterials in these types of sensors, as summarized in Table 2 .

Fluorescence is a property of nanomaterials, especially 0D and 2D, which can be used for analytical purposes. This phenomenon is observed when the electrons of a molecule or nanoparticle return to the ground state, after being excited at a certain wavelength, in a relatively short time (10−9 to 10−7 s) [219]. Many specialized fluorescence techniques are used to understand electronic or charge transfer mechanisms, such as Forster Resonant Energy Transfer (FRET), Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET) and Inner Filter Effect (IFE) [220].

FRET is a non-radiative energy transfer phenomenon that can occur in a system having a fluorescent donor and a light-absorbing acceptor. For the FRET process to occur, it is essential to overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorbance spectrum of the acceptor [221]. Another relevant information about the FRET mechanism is that the donor and acceptor must be close enough, with a distance of about 10–100 Å [222]. This electrodynamic phenomenon involves the chromophore species in different electronically excited states:
(5)
D ∗
+ A
→
D +
A ∗
where
D ∗
is the excited donor chromophore that transfers the photon to the acceptor chromophore
A
in the ground-state and thereby quenches the emission of fluorescence from the donor molecular system [223]. For some fluorescent probes, the small Stockes shift (less than 30 nm) can result in self-quenching problems due to molecular self-absorption. To solve this problem, Chantarasunthon et al. developed a selective sensor based on the FRET mechanism to detect Fe(III). The authors reported a generating of a substantial pseudo-Stokes shift via donor-acceptor energy transfer with LOD of 83 nmol/L [224].

On the other hand, several molecular turn-on sensors were reported for a variety of cations and anions based on PET, since the electron transfer occurred, formed by the cation radical and the anion radical, respectively [220,225]. As in the FRET-based mechanism, there is a PET pair, which in this case is formed by the donor, who assumes the role of ligand, and an acceptor, who will be the fluorophore. With the photoexcitation in the fluorophore, the electron is transferred from its HOMO to the LUMO orbital, resulting in the creation of a hole in the HOMO of the fluorophore. This results in the transfer of the electron present in the HOMO orbital of the ligand to the empty HOMO orbital of the fluorophore, through an internal redox reaction. This whole process results in the prevention of the return of the electron present in the LUMO orbital of the fluorophore to the ground state, resulting in the quenching of fluorescence in the system [226]. It is important to emphasize that the first sensors based on the PET mechanism were restricted to homogeneous systems because photoinduced electron transfer can generate radical ions from neutral materials. To avoid this, the ions produced need to be stabilized by properly oriented solvent dipoles [227].

IFE results from the absorption of the excitation or emission light by absorbers in the detection system when the absorption spectra of the absorbers overlap with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores [228]. The detection system based on IFE has simpler experimental requirements when compared to those based on FRET. Basically, the requirement is spectral overlap between the emission band of the fluorophore species and the absorption band of the quencher [229]. Mondal et al. prepared pyridinic-N-rich carbon dots for detection of diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP). The authors attributed the quenching of fluorescence to the IFE mechanism. From the linear Stern-Volmer plot, the LOD was found to be 84 μmol/L for sensing DCP for the concentration ranging between 3 and 15 mmol/L [230]. Recently, IFE was applied to construct a ‘turn off’ mode up-conversion fluorescence and colorimetric dual-reading detection platform for the detection of glucose and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Under the optimal experimental conditions, the linear ranges of H2O2 and glucose were 2.5–50 and 5–80 μmol/L with LOD of 0.32 and 0.83 μmol/L [231].

Generally, one of these quenching mechanisms can explain changes in the fluorescence spectrum, suggesting a good analyte detection capacity. Then, these phenomena mentioned previously are observed in two categories of fluorescence sensors, which are commonly reported based on their response in the presence of the analytes: when the fluorescence intensity increases, a sensor is termed as off-on, switch-on or turn-on; in contrast, if emission is quenched due to the binding to the analyte, the sensor is termed as on-off, switch-off or turn-off [226]. In the same system, a sensor that uses both strategies with the same nanomaterial is easily obtained. Long Ma et al. synthesized MOFs to recognize Fe3+ and Cr2O7 2− ions via fluorescence suppression (turn-off). In the same work, the authors managed to sense pyridine from the recovery of the fluorescence signal [232]. An et al. applied the fluorescence signal of carbon dots synthesized from the electrolysis of o-phenylenediamine to quantify Fe3+ ions and ascorbic acid. In the first step, Fe3+ ions quenched the system's fluorescence, which increased in the presence of ascorbic acid. The limits of detection (LOD) values obtained by the authors were 0.16 and 0.32 μmol/L for Fe3+ and ascorbic acid, respectively [233].

Liu et al. developed an ‘off-on-off’ fluorescence platform for organophosphate pesticides detection based on boron carbon oxynitride quantum dots and MnO2 nanosheets as quencher [234]. Under optimal experimental conditions, the detection range of paraoxon was 0.1–250 ng/mL, and the LOD was 0.03 ng/mL. In this same perspective, Tang et al. used blue fluorescent carbon dots and MnO2 nanosheets with great ability of fluorescence quenching and scattering to detect H2O2 and glucose [235]. Furthermore, these authors constructed an optical sensing system with two pathways: a turn-off mode and a ratiometric signal, a strategy that will be discussed in greater depth in the next section. In this work, it was related to a dual-mode detection method exhibited with good sensitivity and high selectivity. Satisfactorily, the LODs of glucose detection were 95 and 113 nmol/L for ratiometric mode and turn-off mode, respectively. This excellent result was ensured based on the fluorescence intensity of the sensor mainly generated by MnO2 nanosheets, which are significantly reduced, while the fluorescence intensity quenched by MnO2 nanosheets correspondingly restored in the presence of the analyte.

Swaminathan et al. explored FRET between MoS2 quantum dots and polyaniline for turn-on bovine serum albumin (BSA) sensing [236]. Time-resolved fluorescence measurements show the reduction of lifetime components of MoS2 (donor) upon the increasing concentration of polyaniline (acceptor). The quenched fluorescence of MoS2 in the presence of polyaniline was gradually recovered by the addition of BSA. Fu et al. synthesized carbon dots through hydrothermal treatment of p-dihydroxybenzene and hydrazine hydrate [237]. The fluorescence of carbon dots was quenched significantly and quickly upon addition of Fe3+ and posteriorly. The quenched fluorescence of carbon dots could be selectively turned on by the addition of ampicillin, with LOD of 0.70 μmol/L. Given these results, the authors applied the system in the ampicillin detection in milk, pork, river, tap and mineral water with good average recoveries ranging from 85.4% to 101.4%. Chang et al. used carbon dots with red emission for the fluorescent and colorimetric bimodal sensing of pH [238]. The nanosensor can be utilized to track MnO4 − and pH fluctuations in vivo and in vitro. Besides, evident color changes of RCDs solution under sunlight can be visualized at different pH values, which are more desirable for the fluorescent and colorimetric bimodal sensing of pH.

Atchudan et al. used leftover kiwi (Actinidia deliciosa) fruit peels to synthesize fluorescent kiwi-derived carbon dots (KN-CDs) [239]. Fig. 6 (a) shows the strong fluorescence of the KN-CDs selectively quenched by Fe3+. Quenching behavior showed a linear correlation with the concentrations of Fe3+ ions, within a concentration range of 5–25 μmol/L with the detection limit of 0.95 μmol/L (Fig. 6(b)). Zhang et al. used GQDs as a highly sensitive probe for the sensing of formaldehyde (HCHO) in an aqueous solution [240]. LOD obtained by GQDs was about 0.0515 μg/mL. In addition, the authors successfully applied it to the actual food inspection (Fig. 6(c–i)) and proved to be a selective, sensitive and visualized method to check whether the concentration of HCHO in food exceeds the regulatory limit. To evaluate the applicability of the GQDs fluorescence probe, the performance of GQDs in three common meat samples (pork, chicken and hairtail) was investigated. The extracts of the three foods did not quench the fluorescence of GQDs in normal samples, though they quenched the polluted samples (Fig. 6(c -ii)). A GQDs test paper in the normal sample emits cyan fluorescence under the irradiation of an ultraviolet lamp, and the fluorescence is not quenched (Fig. 6(c-iii). In the contaminated sample, through volatilization, HCHO is transferred from the food to the GQDs fluorescent test paper, quenching the cyan fluorescence of GQDs.

Chen et al. developed a turn-on luminescence resonance energy transfer (LRET) method based on luminous nanorods that were designed for As(III) detection [241]. Biotin-labeled As(III) aptamers were tagged to avidin functionalized luminous nanorods as energy donors, while graphene oxide (GO) acted as the energy acceptor. In Fig. 6(d), the authors proved a high selectivity to this species, as compared to other possible interferents, the sensor was highly selective to As3+ ions. Furthermore, a good linear relationship between the luminescence intensity and concentration of As(III) was obtained in the range of 1–50 ng/mL, with a detection limit of 0.5 ng/mL. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have also been demonstrated to be desired candidates for sensing species due to their tunable composition, framework structure, and functionality. Dong et al. show that cysteine (Cys) can significantly enhance the fluorescence emission of amino-functionalized MOFs suspended in water, with the ability to sense lysine (Lys), arginine (Arg) and histidine (His) in aqueous medium via turn-on fluorescence emission [242]. In another work, Alfé et al., starting from MIL125-(Ti), a 1,4-benzene dicarboxylate (BDC)-based MOF with Ti as metallic center produced mesoporous TiO2 powders containing both anatase and rutile crystalline phases [243]. A challenge related to the use of MOF-derived Ti-based oxides is the optical quantitative detection of molecular oxygen (O2) in gaseous and/or aqueous medium.

Recently, our work group have developed a new sensing platform based on a turn-off sensor to detect nitrite ions [244], where CQDs were synthesized from citric acid, boric acid and branched polyethyleneimine (bPEI) by the one-step hydrothermal route. The obtained nanoparticles showed high QY (44.9%) and, then, were incorporated into a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) matrix film. The resulting nanocomposite was used for field analysis device. Posteriorly, the dripping of aqueous solutions of nitrite ions resulted in the quenching of the fluorescence of the system, allowing the construction of a robust sensing platform. The authors also reported that sensing in solution allowed the selective detection of nitrite ions, with a percentage range from 91.4 to 104% in real meat samples. In addition, Table 3 lists some more examples of turn-on/turn-off sensors with the description of the fluorescent nanoparticles used, and their QY values and main analytical figures of merit.

With the advancement of research in sensing, the development of fluorescent sensors using the intensity ratio of two distinct emission wavelengths as the detection signal has been attracting the attention due to its amazing advantages, including self-calibration and precise quantification [245]. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors, which simultaneously measured two or more fluorescence changes, could efficiently avoid interference of the excitation light source and provide information with the most precision in comparison to single fluorescence probes [246]. QDs and MOFs are some examples of nanomaterials that can be used in this type of sensor. For instance, Chen et al. developed a dual-emission sensor constructed with CdTe quantum dots and Zr-MOF, which was developed for sensing ascorbic acid [247]. The authors showed that this system had appropriate sensitivity, superior selectivity and good accuracy for the detection of ascorbic acid.

There are still a lot of applicability of this type of sensor in practical daily situations. Yue et al. built a new portable smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescence sensor for intelligent and visual detection of malachite green [248]. A portable smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor was successfully designed using fluorescent Al-MOF nanosheet and rhodamine B as fluorescent probes to adjust to the requirement of dye detection. In this same perspective, Li et al. obtained a smartphone-integrated ratiometric fluorescence sensor for visual detection of Cd2+ ions [249]. The limit of 1.1 μg/L for Cd2+ detection is qualified for the safety inspection and a smartphone-based colorimetric assay could achieve visual assay of the analyte in oyster. Hao et al. designed a smartphone-combined dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence probe for specifically and visibly detecting cephalexin [250]. In the probe, blue-emitting fluorescent CDs were synthesized and covered with a layer of silica spacer.

Huang et al. prepared carbon dots, with intrinsic dual-emission fluorescence (d-CDs) at 360 nm (F360) and 530 nm (F530) under excitation of 320 nm, by microwave-assisted heat treatment of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) [251]. Cu2+ could be effectively captured by the d-CDs via coordinating with the nitrogen lone pair of –NH2 to form a complex (Cu2+-OPD) by electron pair sharing. The complex can efficiently quench the fluorescence of F360 by through-bond energy transfer (TBET) while selectively enhancing the fluorescence of F530 by inhibiting the occurrence of PET. Some CDs were utilized to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect Cu2+ with a wide linear range of 0.8–55.0 μmol/L and a low limit of detection (LOD) of 44.63 nmol/L (R2 = 0.9989). Recoveries of 95.8–102.3% with RSD <6.7% were achieved in spiked food samples.

Sotolongo-García et al. optimized a FRET between CDs as energy donors and riboflavin (RF) as an energy acceptor to characterize the main parameters of interaction and sensing this analyte in beverages [252]. Fig. 7 (a) shows the variation in fluorescence spectra of CDs–RF conjugates in the presence of various RF concentrations and the RF calibration curve (average of three repetitions), with a linear concentration range of 0–11 μmol/L, a correlation coefficient of 0.9973, and a LOD of 0.025 μmol/L (inset). Zhai et al. constructed a novel colorimetric and ratiometric fluorescence sensor by using CQDs and o-diaminobenzene (ODB) [253]. The detection principle is shown in Fig. 7(b). Glucose was oxidized to produce gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide under the catalytic action of glucose oxidase. Hydrogen peroxide, as an oxidant, was able to rapidly oxidize ODB with the activity of horseradish peroxidase, producing a fluorescence peak at 550 nm, and the intensity increased with glucose.

Wei et al. obtained blue emission CDs in the one-pot solvothermal method using naringin as precursor [254]. Therefore, CDs were used to develop a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the sensitive analysis of Al3+ with a detection limit of 113.8 nmol/L. As displayed in Fig. 7(c), the fluorescence of the CDs at 420 nm gradually decreases, whereas the fluorescence emission peak at 500 nm appears after the addition of Al3+. The fluorescence intensity ratio (I500/I420) was used to investigate the sensitivity of the CDs probe for Al3+ (Fig. 7(d)). On the other hand, Fig. 7(e) shows a photo that was taken by a smartphone under a 360 nm UV light. The fluorescence of the CDs-Al3+ solution was displaced from blue to green with increasing Al3+ concentration, which was available for the RGB analysis. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of B gradually decreases, whereas the fluorescence intensity of G increases. By considering the ratio between the blue channel and the green channel (G/B) as the signal, a linear trend between the concentration of Al3+ and the G/B value can be obtained (Fig. 7 (f)). Table 4 summarizes the work presented in this section, identifying the band intensity ratio, detection limits and linear range of the developed sensors.

As mentioned in section 2, metals, such as Au, Ag, and Cu, have high charge-carrier concentration and light absorption in the visible and NIR range. Given this characteristic, noble metal-based nanoparticles can support the plasmonic effect [92]. Plasmonic sensors have emerged as a powerful tool in chemical and biological sensing applications and noble metal nanoparticles (AuNPs and AgNPs) are especially suitable for sensing applications, as they possess high extinction coefficients in the visible region [255]. Similar to the plasmon resonance band, the aggregation profile and therefore, the optical readout by UV–Vis spectroscopy depends on the size distribution, shape and dielectric environment [256]. This mechanism can then be exploited in many sensing strategies and the development of new SPR-based sensors.

Xianyu et al. developed a sensor for naked-eye detection of the β-glucuronidase activity with silver deposition on gold nanostars enabling a plasmonic nanosensor [257]. By modulating the silver deposition on gold nanostars, the unique plasmonic property of silver-coated gold nanostars promotes a significant change in the surface plasmon resonance that allows for a plasmonic readout to detect the enzymatic activity. Gu et al. developed an ultrasensitive acetone sensor based on holey zinc oxide nanosheets doped by AuNPs [258]. Au doped holey ZnO nanosheets were prepared by hydrothermal method. Due to the high specific surface area, the small crystallite size of the holy ZnO nanosheets and the catalytic effect of gold nanoparticles, the sensor based on the composite shows ultra-sensitivity and selectivity to acetone (1012.6/100 ppm) and the detection limit is 0.2 ppm. Considering this excellent performance, the sensor was used in medical diagnosis.

A new strategy based on Fenton reaction coupled with AgNPs was designed by Basiri et al. for highly sensitive colorimetric determination of Fe2+, H2O2 and glucose [259]. A selective sensor with a detection limit of 0.54 μmol/L was provided for Fe2+ measuring. On the other hand, the concentration of H2O2 affected both the oxidation of AgNPs and the discoloration of the solution. These conditions were used to measure H2O2 with a LOD as low as 0.032 μmol/L. In other work, Dissanayake et al. detected five organophosphorus pesticides (ethion, fenthion, malathion, parathion and paraoxon-ethyl) with AuNPs and AgNPs [260]. The detection can be monitored by changes in the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) band. Furthermore, each sensor can differentiate between the five organophosphorus pesticides and the detection is visible to the naked eye and the LOD of AgNPs with ethion, fenthion, malathion, and parathion were 9 ppm, 11 ppm, 18 ppm, and 44 ppm, respectively. In addition, Liang et al. have developed a resonance scattering spectral detection of catalase activity using Au@Ag nanoparticle as a probe and coupling catalase catalytic reaction with Fenton reaction and proved the applicability of this technique [261]. In order to organize the data showed in this section, Table 5 presents examples of plasmonic sensors, their SPR bands and analytical figures of merit for the respective detected analytes.

Colorimetric sensors were designed based on the colorimetric method, as it is a simple and rapid technique used to detect certain analytes through chromogenic reactions. In these types of sensors, signal changes can be detected with the naked eye or portable spectrophotometers and even with the digital camera of smartphones [262]. In recent years, smartphones have stood out in works with colorimetric strategies integrated with sensors, due to their portability and ubiquitous availability. Colorimetric sensors have attracted increasing research due to their advantages, for instance, simple operation, safety, high sensitivity and suitability for high-throughput screening applications [263]. In recent years, colorimetric sensors have been widely used to detect various analytes including metal [264,265], anions [266,267] and organic contaminants [268]. Some colorimetric sensors are shown in Table 6 , as well as their main characteristics.

Anwar et al. obtained AuNPs stabilized by a pyrazinium thioacetate ligand: A new colorimetric nanosensor for the detection of heavy metal Pd(II) [269]. The system demonstrated a sensitive and selective spectrophotometric recognition, as well as naked eye indication for recognition of Pd(II) ions, ensuring the colorimetric analysis of Pd(II), which has a distinguished response to decrease of SPR band induced by the aggregation. The nanoprobe displayed a sensitive response to the Pd(II) ions in a wide range of concentrations and pH, with LOD as low as 4.23 μmol/L. There are many works in which dual-functional nanosensors for fluorescent and colorimetric detection of metal ions are generally shown in complex organic-inorganic hybrid nanostructures. Chen et al. fabricate fluorescent carbyne nanocrystals (inorganic nanocrystals) capped with gold nanoparticles (CNCs@AuNPs) via convenient one-step laser ablation in liquids. The authors demonstrated that, with the involvement of ferrous ions, CNCs@AuNPs show fluorescence-quenching and color-producing properties, which it makes them a double-channel platform for chemical and biomedical detection [270].

Zhao et al. reported a method for sulfide anion detection using AgNPs, with dopamine as a reducing and protecting agent [271]. The changes in the AgNPs absorption response depend linearly on the concentration of S2− in the range of 2–15 μmol/L, with a LOD of 0.03 μmol/L. Therefore, a possible mechanism was proposed by the degradation of the structure of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) due to the formation of Ag2S, which consequently precipitated from the dispersion by the reaction between Ag+ and S2− according to the following Eq. (6):

Park et al. developed a colorimetric sensor based on polymer-based core-shell nanoparticles (PCSNPs), which are inexpensive, stable, and exhibit enhanced selectivity [272]. Spherical and uniform poly (styrene-co-maleic anhydride) (PSMA)/polyethyleneimine (PEI) core-shell nanoparticles were prepared and then impregnated with methyl red (MR), bromocresol purple (BCP), or 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) to construct colorimetric sensors for formaldehyde gas. The colorimetric sensor performance was evaluated as shown in Fig. 8 , when the colorimetric sensors incorporated various dyes (BCP, 4-NP, or MR) and PEI ratios were exposed to formaldehyde gas, color changes were observed.

As shown in section 4.1, Liu et al. prepared BCNO QDs and MnO2 nanosheets to turn ‘off-on-off’ for organophosphate pesticides detection via IFE [234]. Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) catalyzes the hydrolysis of acetylcholine (ATCh) to produce thiocholine (TCh). TCh can reductively degrade MnO2 nanosheets to generate Mn2+, thus recovering the fluorescence of BCNO QDs. Meanwhile, the reaction system also showed concentration-dependent visual color changes from colorless to brownish. Huang et al. proposed a facile colorimetric paper sensor using γ-MnOOH nanowires as a degradable nanozyme and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) as a chromogenic indicator for rapid and sensitive screening of organophosphorus pesticides and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity [273]. The concentration of organophosphorus pesticides, as an AChE inhibitor, was measured by the changes in absorbance at 652 nm or blue color of oxTMB products. Such detection platform in the solution state achieved relatively low limits of detection (LODs) for the pesticides omethoate and dichlorvos were 0.35 ng/mL and 0.14 ng/mL, respectively.

Array-based sensing system assembled from various cross-reactive sensors have emerged as a powerful tool for simultaneously identify multiple analytes in the complex environment [218]. Sensor arrays have many advantages over individual sensor elements, including accuracy, diversity, and the capacity for simultaneous detection and discrimination of a series of analytes with similar structures or properties [274]. As well as colorimetric sensors, a wide range of analytes can be sensed by this method, including speciation of metallic ions [275], antibiotics discrimination [276,277], pesticides identification [278,279] and proteins differentiation [280,281]. Table 7 summarizes the main array sensors as well as the algorithms used as statistical analysis tools.

Sun et al. proposed a doubled nanoplasmonic colorimetric sensor array for protein recognition based on carbon nanodots (CDs)-induced reversible aggregation of AuNP with GSH as a regulator [282]. GSH was introduced into each unit containing in situ protein samples. The SPR absorption band of AuNP was substantially affected due to the stronger affinity between AuNP and GSH, producing another three-unit response signal. With this strategy, each sensing element can give two output signals to each target protein, which doubles the number of units from three to six without changing the three-cell configuration, resulting in greatly improved discrimination ability of proteins and identification accuracy of unknown samples. Twelve proteins at 50 nmol/L can be well-discriminated by this novel colorimetric sensor array.

Zhou et al. developed a novel lanthanide-based sensor array to discriminate eleven metal ions [283]. In this work, three lanthanide-based fluorescent probes (Ce-ATP, Tb-GMP, and Eu-BOP) was developed as a novel tongue-mimic chemosensor array for rapid discrimination of multiple metal ions. The three fluorescent probes exhibit different binding affinities for metal ions, resulting in diverse fluorescence behaviors and distinct fluorescence response patterns. The patterns can act as ‘fingerprints’ for the identification of multiple metal ions via Principal Component Analysis (PCA) technology. PCA is probably the most commonly used multivariate technique, in which the correlated original variables are transformed into sets of linearly uncorrelated variables called principal components. This procedure reduces the dimensionality of data with a minimum loss of information [284,285].

Cao et al. obtained four kinds of silver nanoclusters for the identification of heavy metal ions [286]. The sensor array was used to identify seven types of ions: Pb2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Co2+ and Ni2+. The fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of the ions was used as a response signal and the vast amount of data obtained can be reduced using the PCA tool. The authors observed that it was possible to clearly separate the seven types of ions in a PCA scatter plot in a concentration range of 15–800 μmol/L at pH = 7. In addition, the sensor was also able to detect the seven ions in a tap water sample, indicating their potential for practical applications in the environment [286].

In addition to PCA, a linear discriminant analysis (LDA) technique is customarily applied in a sensor array. Ronald A. Fisher proposed a criterion for maximizing the separation between two or more classes of samples that became known as Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) [287], which searches for a linear function of the variable in multivariate space. Carneiro et al. used CQDs obtained from the hydrothermal treatment of riboflavin and AgNPs, which efficiently quench the fluorescence of CQDs due to a Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET). The results were analyzed by linear discriminant analysis (LDA), obtaining different response patterns against pesticide concentrations, with a confidence level of 95% [288]. It was verified that in pepper extract, the sensibility of the sensing strategy was 250 ng/mL.

Inspired by the success of the strategy developed in the previous work, Carneiro's group decided to apply this methodology in other later works. In this turn, the authors obtained CQDs from a natural source (American seed Caelsalpinia pulcherrima) to discriminate food additives commonly found in industrialized products [289]. In this work, five additives were correctly identified only by the LDA algorithm and the sensing strategy successfully identified all additives tested in a pickled olives sample with 95% of confidence, in which 100% of combinations were correctly identified based on the classification matrix. The success in clusters LDA separation is based on the standard distance of Mahalanobis: the larger the distance between aggregates, the lower the probability of wrong identification of analytes [290].

Zhang et al. developed a fluorescent sensor array for heavy metal ions discrimination based on a single gold nanocluster (AuNC) probe [291]. The sensor was prepared using 2-mercapto-1-methylimidazole (MMI) as a ligand and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) as a dispersing agent. The fluorescence emission of PVP/MMI-AuNC was used as a response signal in the sensing of Mn2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Sn2+, Fe2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+. The authors observed that the different ions produced different effects on the photoluminescence of PVP/MMI-AuNC and generated distinct fluorescent responses at 512 and 700 nm. This phenomenon provided the complete separation of the seven ions, either in the buffer solution or in water samples, using hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) and LDA [291].

Shahrajabian and Reza Hormozi-Nezhad introduced a novel, simple and sensitive nanoparticle-based chemiluminescence (CL) sensor array for discrimination of biothiols (e.g., cysteine, glutathione and glutathione disulfide) [292]. In this work, the effects of these biomolecules on the luminol–AgNO3–AuNPs and the luminol–H2O2–(AgNPs/CdTe QDs) CL systems have been investigated. As shown in Fig. 9 (a–d), by increasing GSH concentration, the CL intensity of luminol–H2O2–QDs and luminol–H2O2–AgNPs increased linearly, while the CL intensity of luminol-AgNO3-(Cit-, BH4 −) AuNPs decreased. Biothiols in wide concentration ranges were exposed to different types of NPs and the signals were recorded. The patterns were subjected to LDA and the information was transformed into canonical scores, which were visualized as a well-clustered two-dimensional plot as shown in Fig. 9(e). The authors successfully discriminated biothiols, for GSSG and GSH in the concentration range of 5.0–800.0 μmol/L and for Cys in the concentration range of 25.0–100.0 μmol/L by utilizing the training matrix generated from CL patterns.

On the other hand, Support Vector Machines (SVMs) showed better generalization performance and, therefore, have been applied in different domains, including sensor arrays. Unlike artificial neural networks (ANNs), which minimize the empirical risk, SVMs implement the structural risk minimization principle [293].

Xu et al. obtained CQDs for the determination of four TETs based on IFE mechanism [277]. A dual-channel fluorescence sensor array based on two CQDs was produced to distinguish between four TETs and a distinct fluorescence variation pattern (I/I0) was produced when CQDs interacted with the four TETs. This pattern was analyzed by LDA and SVM. The results proved that SVM can achieve the same accurate classification and prediction as LDA, and considering its additional advantages, it can be used as an optional supplementary method for data processing, thereby expanding the data processing field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of thin films for nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: Thin films have been key points in developing new sensors in different fields, from optical sensors to gas detection, using nanomaterials. In fact, thin films are usually obtained from materials or nanomaterials, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) or CQDs), and 2-dimensional nanostructures, like graphene and molybdenum disulfide (MoS2), have been investigated for sensing applications due to their unique advantageous properties [294]. For example, Wang et al. used N-doped carbon-dots to be embedded in polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) thin films [295].

In another work, Segkos et al. obtained a CD-based fluorescent nanocomposite for application in thin film deposition with SiO2 as substrates, ensuring that the films were dissolution-resistant and processable [296]. Optical lithography and O2 plasma etching are utilized to pattern the deposited films in the desired shapes and dimensions and a solid-state relative humidity sensor is prepared on the SiO2 substrate. Rivadeneyra et al. developed based on interdigitated capacitive electrodes screen printed on a flexible transparent polyethylene terephthalate (PETP) film (Fig. 10 (a)) [297]. This work presented an innovative application of CDs nanoparticles as a sensing layer for relative humidity detection.

Kwuan et al. evaluated the dispersal of CDs in low cost, biocompatible and transparent poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) [298]. The nanocomposite films presented highly photoluminescent properties with the addition of CDs and the sensor showed promising results for detecting tartrazine, ensuring food quality control at an analyte concentration as low as 10 μmol L-1. Zhao et al. dispersed CQDs in polymer matrices to form luminescent films [299]. The authors fabricated free-standing flexible PVA films with photoluminescent CQDs, as shown in Fig. 10(b). The simulated results show that the evanescent wave at the flat PVA/air interface can be effectively scattered out of the film.

Omar et al. developed a sensitive detection of dengue virus type 2 E-proteins (DENV-2 E-proteins) which was performed in the range of 0.08–0.5 pmol/L [300]. The specificity, sensitivity, binding affinity, and selectivity of the SPR sensor were then studied. SPR measurements were conducted based on Kretschmann configuration and Fig. 10(c) is a schematic illustration representing the proposed sensor with the introduction of DENV-2 E-proteins. Results proved that the variation of the sensing layer due to different spin speeds, time incubation, and concentration provided a better interaction between the analyte and sensing layer.

Additionally, Kim et al. obtained a highly sensitive gas sensors that play a significant role in industry and daily life, and MoS2 has emerged as one of the most promising two-dimensional nanomaterials for gas sensor technology [301]. The schematic image of Fig. 11 (a) shows the preparation of MoS2 film on a silicon (Si)/silicon dioxide (SiO2) substrate, where molybdenum (Mo) metal was pre-deposited at room temperature and then sulfurized at 750 °C in a chamber with a gas mixture of hydrogen sulfide (H2S), hydrogen (H2), and argon (Ar). The sulfurization induced a transition from the Mo film into a layered MoS2 structure (bottom of Fig. 11(a)). The gas sensor arrays exhibited good switching capabilities and must be readily integrated with additional circuitry for monitoring applications. The thickness of the MoS2 film was measured as 1.64 nm using atomic force microscopy (AFM), which may suggest two MoS2 layers (Fig. 11(b)).

Ricci et al. developed a thermodynamic sensor that can detect a multitude of explosives in the vapor phase at the parts-per-trillion (ppt) level [302]. The sensors rely on the catalytic decomposition of the explosive and specific oxi-reduction reactions between the energetic molecule and metal oxide catalyst. The thermodynamic sensor was constructed on ultrathin, 20 μm thick yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) ribbons measuring 1.6 cm × 0.7 cm. A schematic of the various layers comprising the thermodynamic sensor is shown in Fig. 11 (c). The detection of a variety of peroxide-based explosives as well as nitroaromatics and nitrates at the ppt level was reported. Most notably, the detection of plastic explosives was achieved at concentrations of 0.5 ppt.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is widely present in the modern world in computers, mobile phones, cars, search engines, marketing, politics, economy, electronic games, industry, household appliances, aerospace, research and development (R&D) of drugs [303]. The main reasons for the interest in AI are the evolution of technology in processors and batteries, which allowed mobile devices, and communications, that allowed the connection between devices to become a network of data collection and processing, that culminates in the Internet of Things (IoT) which in turn allows for more sturdy output and to determine causality of data that would otherwise be interpreted as not related.

The definition of AI varies depending on the context. However, a simple and wide concept is to make machines act in an intelligent way [304], generally to achieve a goal, as in the development of a drug, or react to an everchanging environment (express intelligent behavior), as in electronic games or chess which an AI reacts to the actions of a human player aiming to win. The advantages of AIs over traditional programming are immense, most noted being that typical software has outputs according to a set of instructions, in which AIs can evolve and develop themselves and even predict data [305], since it is developing a model or function to fit the data (input), it is easier to apply and extrapolate a function that describes the behavior of data than to run and verify each point of data (that needs to be stored, consuming space) for each user entry.

The use of AIs in chemistry and related areas of research in recent years has been increased rapidly. According to Baum et al. the ratio of AI publications to the total number of publications in 2015 was close to 0.2%, and in 2020 the ratio was close to 1.1%, characterizing it as a global trend, which was potentialized with the COVID-19 pandemic, being used to develop drugs, diagnosis and track of the disease [305]. An example of the use in the COVID-19 pandemics was reported by Ucar and Korkmaz who developed an algorithm to detect the disease in X-ray images as an alternative to more expensive and labor-consuming molecular tests, as the X-ray images of infected patients present ground-glass opacities in the lungs where a pattern recognition can be made (by an experienced doctor or an algorithm) allowing the disease to be detected rapidly and accurately. The performance reached, at the date of publication, state-of-the-art with 98.3% of accuracy, in the infected cases, and 100% to differentiate between COVID-19 and other conditions with similar X-ray images [306].

Another example is linear regression as described by Bian et al. in the sensing of Hg2+ utilizing carbon nanotubes (CNT) field-effect transistor (FET), in which the high sensitivity of the sensor may saturate the signal depending on the concentration, so a method of calibration by machine learning of linear regression was applied, together with other algorithms to compensate for non-linearity, achieving a model that allows for a greater range of concentration of the sensor and provided higher insight of the sensing mechanism [307].

In sensors, the use of AI and machine learning may be used in general for the classification, data analysis and pattern recognition, as shown in Fig. 12 illustrating a typical sensing of a complex sample that translates to a huge quantity of variables, both dependent and independent. The signal is processed, classified and recognized to isolate the signal from the interest analyte. An analogy can be made with statistical methods such as LDA as a classificatory tool, as it can reduce the total signal to signals of interest or not, serving as an input to the pattern recognition, which detects the signal of interest that is the aim of this particular AI, providing feedback for data analysis and classifications to refine the accuracy of both. This example shows a disadvantage of AIs, which is the amount of data it needs to work properly, a small amount of data would not efficiently exclude noise [308].

There are three forms of machine learning: Reinforcement Learning, Supervised Learning and Unsupervised Learning. The first is similar to the classical Skinner conditioning chamber, proportionally rewarding actions that put them closer to the predetermined long-term goal (positive reinforcement) and punishing what deviates from the goal (negative reinforcement) [309]. Like Skinner rats, algorithms can be trained this way, like machines that play chess.

Supervised Learning means that part of the data is known (labeled) and indicated to the algorithm (training set) that learns to identify the label, so a real unidentified (unlabeled) data can be compared to the training set, with an accuracy directly proportional to the robustness of the training. Thus, the bigger and more diverse the data known to train, the better results can be achieved. An example is Google reCAPTCHA, a google tool that is used to protect webpages from bots and help train algorithms to identify words, house numbers, signposts, hydrant and traffic lights.

Unsupervised Learning, as opposed to the supervised, receives unknown data to try to analyze the set and to cluster (group data with similar characteristics, such as a taxonomist), associate (e.g., characteristic A is always followed by behavior B), anomaly detection (such as a flying car) and autoencoder (translate the data to a compressed code and translate it back to the original data and compare both). Although the lack of a base reference affects accuracy, this method can be improved using together with other tools, statistical or supervised learning, in a hybrid semi-supervised in which the data is a mix of labeled and unlabeled, greatly improving accuracy, typically applied in hospitals for X-ray analysis, CT scans and MRIs [310,311].

In sensing applications, the use of AIs with machine learning can be applied to optical sensors, nanomaterials synthesis optimization [312], properties prediction [313], mechanism of interaction sensor-analytes [314], data analysis, noise removal, pattern recognition and classification [306], which is highly desirable when working with large amounts of data, for instance, the detection of biomarkers in blood samples. This complex biological matrix may interact with the sensors producing a high order of magnitude number of signals that is difficult for a manual operator to interpret or extract useful information.

An approach to the selectivity of a sensor is the use of an electronic nose (e-nose), which is a state-of-the-art term that defines a system of different electronic sensors, each for an analyte or a group of them. Thai et al. proposed the detection and quantification of different gases (acetone, ammonia, ethanol, hydrogen and hydrogen sulfide). The data was fed to a Support Vector Machine, a type of machine learning algorithm that allows classification and regression in supervised data. The SVM was fitted to a training data set, obtaining a perfect classification of each gas for both sensors types, and the regression was used to determine the concentration, which the overall error is in the range of 18 ± 5.1% (mean error of 18.3%) for Ag-based sensor and 29.27 ± 17.9% (mean error of 19.5%) due to a very high error in H2S. Thus a system that uses both sensors can further minimize errors, except when the probability of H2S is high to avoid elevating the error in Pt-based sensors [315].

In the context of a nose-sensor approach, Cruz et al. developed a chemical nose, that is, a set of different sensor materials, in contrast to electronic sensors in e-nose, which interacts and emits different signals with all analytes in the sample, allowing for a well-defined differentiation in complex samples with numerous, independent and dependent, variables. The synthesized sensors were CQDs with metals to tune the surface to analyze hemoglobin, myoglobin, cytochrome C and Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) through fluorescence, whose data obtained were analyzed by LDA to classify, successfully discriminating each protein without error [67].

The development of materials can be expensive and time-consuming to obtain the optimized properties for a determined task, as it contains a great number of variables (synthesis route, temperature, solvent, purification, surface modification, band gap, tensile strength, stability, biocompatibility, among other conditions). To improve the overall development of sensor materials, Epps et al. produced an autonomous microflow system to optimize perovskite QDs, which made syringes that function as reservoirs and pumps of tuning reagents, QD base and perfluorinated oil. In this work, the computer compares the results with the desired properties and if it deviates from the desired, the computer modifies the flow rate in the system and repeats, learning the influence of each variable in the input it receives, by an algorithm called Artificial Neural Network (ANN) [312,316]. Fu et al. work is similar, except that data entry was from literature reports and determining the main variables of the synthesis [317].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing speed, sensing reversibility and test reproducible with fluorescent nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: After a presentation on the main sensing strategies using 0D, 1D and 2D nanomaterials, it is important to know certain parameters to optimizer the development of sensors. Therefore, in this section, we will list some relevant factors that must be taken into account when proposing selective sensing with high sensitivity of metals and organic molecules. These parameters ensure the evaluating the quality of the adopted sensing strategy, including sensing speed, reuse/recycle tests and reproducibility. In summary, all these experiments can be explained based on changes in the chemical structure of the surface of the nanoparticle labeled as fluorescent probe. For example, when this reversibility is observed in carbon dots, variations in fluorescence intensity can be attributed to the protonation and deprotonation of amine groups on the surface of the nanoprobe [318,319].

The first parameter considered is the contact time of the nanoprobe with the analyte, i.e., the sensing speed. In this regard, the detection of certain metallic species can occur in a few minutes, as long there is a propitious interaction with the surface of the nanomaterial. For instance, Wang et al. synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) for Ag+ detection and studied the influence of reaction time [320]. The authors observed that after the addition of the Ag+ ions solution to N-CDs suspension, the fluorescence intensity decreased drastically, becoming stable after 3 min of mixing. Cai and collaborators developed a fiber-optic-based sensing platform containing CQDs for real-time Fe3+ sensing [321]. In this work, the fluorescence stability was observed after 6 s. This rapid response was attributed to the porous structure of the hydrogel containing CQDs and used to make the fiber. In addition, the authors obtained an LOD of 0.6 μmol/L for the platform, slightly higher than the value of 0.4 μmol/L obtained for sensing Fe3+ in solution.

Another interesting experiment related to stability is the application of fluorescent nanoprobes as pH sensors. Korah et al. evaluated the fluorescence intensity of CDs over a pH range of 3–10. Fluorescence was maintained over six uninterrupted cycles of acidification and alkalization [318]. Due to the practical application of a probe, its reversibility is extremely important. In order to verify the reversibility, Emami et al. conducted the EDTA titration to verify the reversibility of N-CDs-Fe3+ species [319]. In this work, the fluorescence intensity was quenched after the addition of Fe3+ ions to the N-CDs colloidal suspension. When EDTA solution was added to the N-CDs-Fe3+ complex, the fluorescence intensity was restored completely. These results illustrate that Fe3+ in the N-CDs-Fe3+ complex has been chelated by EDTA. This process was repeated for at least three cycles to confirm that N-CDs was a reusable nanoprobe for detecting Fe3+ ions.

Chen et al. also evaluated the reversibility in the detection of Hg(II) with nitrogen, sulfur co-doped carbon dots [322]. To investigate these parameters, the authors used meso-2,3-dimercaptosuccinic acid (DMSA) as an important role in treatment of heavy metal poisoning, such as mercury. DMSA is a strong Hg2+ chelator due to the two S-atom placed in juxta position. Thus, Hg2+ can be removed from the surface of T–NS–CDs by forming Hg–S bond with DMSA (DMSA-Hg2+). Consequently, the aggregates would be dissociated, resulting in the fluorescence restoration of the T–NS–CDs. Mogharbel et al. developed a turn-off fluorescent sensor for acetone from rice straw-derived carbon dots immobilized onto textile cotton mask [323]. After each acetone cycle, the emission intensity was measured and recorded at 439 nm. In the absence and presence of acetone gas, there were no noticeable variations in emission intensities. This indicates a high level of durability and reversibility.

Reproducibility is another relevant parameter for sensor application in a real sample. This factor is evaluated based on recovery percentages after spiked the analyte to the sample. For example, Thara et al. synthesized C3N4 dots for Hg2+ and tetracycline sensing. The sensing efficiency was tested on tap and lake water samples [324]. The recovery percentage of Hg2+, and tetracycline in the different water samples were obtained in the range of 98.7–100.58%. Nemati et al. developed a fluorescent turn on sensing of caffeine in food sample based on sulfur-doped CQDs [325]. The relative recovery values of 94.6–103.8% were obtained from spiked samples with three repeated measurements. The results demonstrate the applicability of the procedure for the detection of caffeine in real samples. Huang et al. obtained nitrogen-doped carbon dots for turn-on sensing of ATP based on aggregation induced emission enhancement effect [326]. The method proposed exhibited simple and easy preparation of nanoprobe, quick response (3 min) and performed successfully as a ‘turn-on’ sensor for detection of ATP in the tablet with a recovery of 100.1–106.9%.

Given the work presented, it can be inferred that fluorescent nanomaterials can satisfactorily contribute to the development of alternative analytical methodologies. Such strategies can be improved and evaluated, depending on the chemical nature of the fluorescent probe and of the analyte. Detection in real samples also becomes an important step towards confirming the reproducibility and reversibility of the proposed method and evaluating the feasibility of the application.

Encouraged by the great advances in nanomaterials science and technology, we present throughout this review several optical sensing applications. The development of nanomaterials is a target of nanotechnology and fantastic benefits can be achieved when investigating the properties of these advanced materials. One of the main properties, photoluminescence, can be a powerful tool for developing optical sensors. In addition, when investigating the properties of semiconductors and improving the surface area of nanomaterials, gas sensors are easily developed. Herein, these applications were systematically listed, after a brief description of 0D, 1D and 2D nanomaterials and their respective synthesis methods.

Regarding the synthesis methodologies, the literature has presented a wide range of possibilities for obtaining nanomaterials at low cost and following the principles of green chemistry. Nowadays, as important as developing a practical sensor that makes life easier for analysts from different areas is to develop a nanomaterial with minimal environmental impacts. In this perspective, linking the synthesis method to the most suitable precursor, nanomaterials such as nanoparticles, nanowires, nanotubes and nanosheets are obtained on a laboratory scale. This also envisages the possibility of expanding this production scale and a great challenge ahead is faced by chemists and materials scientists: how to develop nanomaterials in an industrial scale? New synthetic routes have been developed and their feasibility must be assessed. Among the general synthesis methods, hydrothermal stands out for its simplicity and obtaining 0D nanomaterials with excellent optical properties, as described in section 2. However, other methods can be combined, such as microwave and ultrasound irradiation, to obtain nanomaterials in other dimensions.

The section 3 of this review was dedicated to exploring the newest in the field of sensors. In this case, the different types of sensors were explored, using the nanomaterials previously described. Thus, the reader can critically perceive that there are a very wide range of possibilities, which was beyond the scope of this text. More specifically, the turn-on and turn-off sensor strategies were highlighted as one of the main tools for developing sensors based solely on the fluorescence mechanism of fluorescent nanoparticles, such as CQDs. However, it is important to emphasize that the spectra themselves can be used to extract all possible information in array sensors. Such sensors are usually developed with fluorescent nanoparticles, but noble metallic nanoparticles that present the SPR phenomenon take up more and more space in this type of sensor.

It is also impossible to develop array sensors without the mastery of pattern recognition methods based on algorithms such as PCA and LDA, which are essential for effective, fast and robust sensing. In addition to these algorithms, other methods such as partial least squares regression (PLS) have been identified as excellent strategies for multivariate calibration and simultaneous detection of several analytes. Furthermore, to make the sensors more practical, it is still possible to migrate nanomaterials to the solid phase and thus thin films are developed and applied directly as sensors. In this case, it is noteworthy that there are possibilities for applications of optical sensors, with 0D nanomaterials (fluorescent nanoparticles and PNPs) and gas sensors, including 1D and 2D nanomaterials.

Finally, we believe that a comprehensive and rich literature review can contribute to advances in developing new optical sensors and new sensing strategies based on photoluminesce mechanisms. Several analytes still need nanoprobes to be detected. There are still fields to be explored from simple molecular structures to macromolecules. In other cases, analysis methodologies or sensors already exist but they need to be improved. Given this panorama, 0D, 1D and 2D nanomaterials are excellent candidates to be explored by future generations of engineers, scientists and chemists dedicated to the advancement of nanotechnology.
